what_it_does: An integration test that launches the MCP server over stdio, connects a real MCP client, composes a two-node graph (constant -> wordcount), runs it, and asserts the self-grown wordcount node returns 5 words.
observations: It is an end-to-end use test, not a mocked unit test.
Uses stdio_client and ClientSession from the MCP SDK to talk to the running server.
Hardcodes the graph id 'mcp-use-demo' and deletes its persisted JSON before running.
Invokes the MCP tools list_types, create_node, connect, run_graph, and get_results.
Asserts that 'wordcount' appears in the type list and that the result for the wordcount node equals the string '5'.
Imports fabric.config as fcfg to locate STORE_DIR for graph persistence.
Includes a small helper _text that extracts and parses JSON from the first content block of tool results.
standouts: End-to-end proof of the MCP agent face using a real stdio client.
Explicitly verifies the self-grown 'wordcount' node is visible and functional.
conventions: Uses a top-level docstring with a run command and purpose statement.
Follows the __main__ guard pattern and returns an exit code via sys.exit(asyncio.run(main())).
Uses sys.path.insert to make the parent package importable.
Names the helper with a leading underscore.
concerns: Hardcodes the virtualenv path '.venv/bin/python' and server path 'mcp_face/server.py'.
Deletes the persisted graph file directly instead of through an MCP tool or API.
No error handling around MCP calls or JSON parsing failures beyond the helper fallback.
Assertions are brittle: exact string '5' and exact type name 'wordcount'.
notes: A runnable integration smoke test that proves the MCP agent face can actually compose and execute a graph with a self-grown node.
questions: How is the 'wordcount' node registered in the server — is it built-in, self-grown, or loaded from the store?
Is the server expected to already be installed in .venv, or should it be started differently?
Why delete the graph file directly rather than using a tool to reset or remove it?
What happens if the server is already running or the stdio transport fails to start?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the docstring states it proves the MCP agent face is operable in real use by composing and running a graph with the self-grown wordcount node, and the code does exactly that.
intention_signals: Demonstrate that the MCP server is not merely registering tools but is actually usable by an external agent client to construct and execute a meaningful graph using a substrate-grown capability.
novelty: It treats the MCP server as a black-box agent interface and verifies self-grown node behavior through real client interaction rather than internal unit tests.
contribution: A runnable integration test that exercises the MCP tool surface end-to-end and verifies an external client can compose and execute a graph using the self-grown wordcount node.
summary_for_embedding: A stdio-based MCP client integration test that connects to the running server, composes a constant-to-wordcount graph, runs it, and asserts the self-grown wordcount node returns '5'.
intention_for_embedding: To prove the MCP agent face works in real use by having a live client build and execute a graph with the self-grown wordcount node.

symbols.does: Extracts and parses the JSON text from the first content block of an MCP tool result.
symbols.description: A small helper that unwraps MCP tool responses: it takes a result object, reads the text of its first content block, attempts json.loads, and returns the parsed object or the raw text if parsing fails.

symbols.does: Runs the end-to-end MCP integration test by composing and executing a constant-to-wordcount graph.
symbols.description: Async test driver that removes any prior persisted graph for the hardcoded graph id, spawns the MCP server over stdio, initializes a ClientSession, calls list_types, create_node, connect, run_graph, and get_results, and asserts that the self-grown wordcount node is visible and produces the result '5'.
